foundingEvent.title: The Founding Charter
foundingEvent.theme: linear vs circular metabolism; the waste hierarchy
foundingEvent.brief: Your city is young, but it's growing fast — new families arrive every week and the bins are filling quicker than anyone expected. The council gathers for its very first big decision: *how should this city deal with its rubbish, as a rule?* Everyone is watching. Whatever you decide today will shape the roads, factories, habits and budgets of the city for years to come. There's no going back to a blank page after this.
foundingEvent.caseFact: A city's "metabolism" can be linear (resources flow in, waste flows out and is discarded) or circular (waste is fed back as a resource). The waste hierarchy ranks strategies from most preferred (reduce) to least (landfill).

entry.theme: linear vs circular metabolism; the waste hierarchy

actions.label: Just bury it — keep it cheap
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest option, so you keep money for other things The land fills up and runs out
actions.plainLabel: Just bury it — keep it cheap
actions.plainMeaning: Collect the rubbish and bury it in a tip. The simplest, cheapest way to start.
pros: Cheapest option, so you keep money for other things
quick and easy to set up
makes space problems disappear for now.
cons: The land fills up and runs out
buried rubbish pollutes soil, air and water over time
you're building lazy "throw-it-away" habits that are hard to undo later.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city — do you treat waste as rubbish to get rid of, or as a resource to reuse?

actions.label: Burn it for electricity
actions.resultExplain: Shrinks the rubbish pile dramatically Expensive to build
actions.plainMeaning: Build a high-tech furnace that burns rubbish and turns the heat into power.
pros: makes electricity you can use or sell
needs far less land than a tip.
cons: the chimney emits pollution that worries nearby residents
you still have to deal with the toxic ash left behind.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city — do you treat waste as rubbish to get rid of, or as a resource to reuse?

actions.label: Sort and recycle it
actions.resultExplain: Turns waste into useful materials and income Costs more to set up
actions.plainMeaning: Ask people to separate their rubbish so materials can be recovered and sold or reused.
pros: Turns waste into useful materials and income
builds a city that bounces back from shocks.
cons: only works if residents actually sort properly
the payoff builds slowly, not overnight.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city — do you treat waste as rubbish to get rid of, or as a resource to reuse?

actions.label: Stop the waste before it starts
actions.resultExplain: The most powerful fix — rubbish you prevent costs nothing to handle The most expensive and slowest to show results
actions.plainLabel: Stop the waste before it starts
actions.plainMeaning: Push to reduce packaging and throwaway goods so less rubbish is ever created.
pros: The most powerful fix — rubbish you prevent costs nothing to handle
biggest long-term win for the environment
positions you as a zero-waste leader.
cons: The most expensive and slowest to show results
businesses and shoppers resist new rules
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city — do you treat waste as rubbish to get rid of, or as a resource to reuse?

justify.question: Why does this founding choice matter so much for sustainability?
options: It sets whether the city runs a linear (discard) or circular (resource) metabolism, shaping all later outcomes

roundEvents.title: The Convenience Trap
roundEvents.brief: Walk down any high street and everything is wrapped in plastic — fruit in trays, drinks in double layers, snacks in pouches inside boxes. Shops love it because shiny packaging sells, but your bins are overflowing overnight. Residents are starting to notice the mountains of wrapping on collection day.
roundEvents.caseFact: Refrigeration and global transport made convenience packaging routine — packaging is now a marketing tool, not just protection.

actions.label: Charge a small fee on excess packaging
actions.resultExplain: Cuts rubbish at the source Shops and shoppers grumble about higher prices
actions.plainLabel: Charge a small fee on excess packaging
actions.plainMeaning: Make shops pay a little for wasteful wrapping, nudging them to use less.
cons: Shops and shoppers grumble about higher prices
the benefit takes time to show.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

actions.label: Run a 'bring your own bag/cup' campaign
actions.resultExplain: Cheap and popular Only some people join in
actions.plainLabel: Run a 'bring your own bag/cup' campaign
actions.plainMeaning: Encourage people to refuse single-use packaging voluntarily.
cons: the effect is small and slow.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

actions.label: Do nothing, just collect more
actions.resultExplain: No backlash Rubbish keeps climbing
actions.plainMeaning: Leave shops alone and keep picking up the extra rubbish.
pros: keeps shops and shoppers happy today.
cons: you've locked in a throwaway culture.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

justify.question: Why is reducing packaging "better" than recycling it?
options: It prevents the waste and its disposal cost from ever existing

roundEvents.title: Built to be Binned
roundEvents.brief: Cheap gadgets and clothes are everywhere, and they're designed to be tossed, not fixed — when a phone battery dies, it's cheaper to buy a new phone than repair it. Landfills are filling with stuff that barely got used. People shrug; that's just how things are now.
roundEvents.caseFact: Many industries rely on built-in obsolescence — replacement is cheaper than repair, driving constant waste.

actions.label: Fund repair cafés and reuse shops
actions.resultExplain: Extends the life of products Costs money up front
actions.plainLabel: Fund repair cafés and reuse shops
actions.plainMeaning: Set up places where people can fix and re-sell things instead of binning them.
cons: only works if people choose to use them.
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

actions.label: Pass a 'right to repair' rule
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the root cause Manufacturers lobby hard against it
actions.plainMeaning: Force makers to design products that can actually be fixed.
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

actions.label: Just collect the extra rubbish
actions.resultExplain: Keeps streets tidy now Treats the symptom, not the cause
actions.plainMeaning: Expand collection to cope with all the discarded goods.
cons: Treats the symptom, not the cause
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

justify.question: What does "built-in obsolescence" do to a city's waste stream?
options: Continuously enlarges it as products are replaced not repaired

roundEvents.title: Lockdown Leftovers
roundEvents.brief: Food delivery has exploded — every meal arrives in a stack of plastic tubs, bags and cutlery. In just two months the city has buried the equivalent of dozens of bus-loads of extra plastic. It's convenient, but the bins tell the story.
roundEvents.caseFact: Singapore households generated +1,334 tonnes of plastic during a two-month lockdown from deliveries.

actions.label: Launch a reusable container scheme
actions.resultExplain: Cuts packaging waste for good Needs a return-and-wash system to work
actions.plainMeaning: Set up returnable tubs so meals don't need throwaway packaging.
cons: Needs a return-and-wash system to work
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

actions.label: Add recycling bins at food courts
actions.resultExplain: Captures materials if people sort right Lots of food-soiled plastic can't be recycled
actions.plainLabel: Add recycling bins at food courts
actions.plainMeaning: Put clearly-labelled bins where the takeaway waste is created.
pros: Captures materials if people sort right
cons: Lots of food-soiled plastic can't be recycled
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

actions.label: Send it all to the tip
actions.resultExplain: No effort or cost now. Plastic lasts centuries
actions.plainLabel: Send it all to the tip
actions.plainMeaning: Just bury the extra packaging.
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

justify.question: Why did delivery culture raise the footprint so fast?
options: More packaging per meal, mostly non-recyclable plastic

roundEvents.title: Edge City
roundEvents.brief: A brand-new neighbourhood is rising at the city's edge — homes are filling up before the bin lorries even have a route out there. Residents are already leaving bags on the kerb with nowhere for them to go. You can shape how this suburb deals with waste from day one.
roundEvents.caseFact: Urban sprawl pushes infrastructure outward; fringe areas often lack basic services first.

actions.label: Build full sorting + collection from day one
actions.resultExplain: Designs good habits in from the start Most expensive option up front.
actions.plainLabel: Build full sorting + collection from day one
actions.plainMeaning: Give the new suburb proper recycling and collection immediately.
pros: Designs good habits in from the start
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

actions.label: Basic bin collection only
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper Locks in throwaway habits
actions.plainMeaning: Just send lorries to pick up mixed rubbish.
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

actions.label: Let residents sort it out themselves
actions.resultExplain: Saves money today. Illegal dumping and resentment grow
actions.plainLabel: Let residents sort it out themselves
actions.plainMeaning: Leave the new suburb to manage its own waste for now.
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

justify.question: Why design waste systems before a district is built?
options: Retrofitting habits and infrastructure later is far harder and costlier
It is required by law everywhere

roundEvents.title: Streets of Refuse
roundEvents.brief: In the older, crowded districts the bin lorries simply can't keep up. Rubbish piles on corners, sometimes catching fire in the heat, and rats and flies are moving in. Families living there are getting sick, and they're angry.
roundEvents.caseFact: The 2012 World Bank report found 30–60% of urban waste in lower-income countries is uncollected.

actions.label: Invest in more lorries and routes
actions.resultExplain: Quickly removes the health hazard Costs money
actions.plainLabel: Invest in more lorries and routes
actions.plainMeaning: Expand the collection fleet to clear the backlog.
cons: you still have to dispose of all that waste somewhere.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

actions.label: Hire local informal collectors
actions.resultExplain: Cheap and fast Needs organising and support to be safe and fair.
actions.plainMeaning: Pay the people already collecting waste by cart to do it officially.
pros: boosts recycling and gives people fair work
cons: Needs organising and support to be safe and fair.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

actions.label: Leave it for now
actions.resultExplain: Saves money this year. Disease spreads
actions.plainMeaning: Don't act; focus the budget elsewhere.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

justify.question: Why is uncollected waste a health emergency, not just an eyesore?
options: It breeds disease vectors and contaminates water

roundEvents.title: Sort It Out
roundEvents.brief: Your team proposes a city-wide scheme: give every household separate bins for paper, glass, plastic and food scraps, and make sorting the easy, normal thing to do. It could transform how the city handles waste — if people get on board.
roundEvents.caseFact: Copenhagen made source separation an easy default at household level for paper, glass, metal, plastic, organics.

actions.label: Full kerbside sorting for every home
actions.resultExplain: Big jump in recycling Expensive to launch
actions.plainLabel: Full kerbside sorting for every home
actions.plainMeaning: Roll out separate bins citywide and collect each stream.
cons: flops if households don't sort properly.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

actions.label: Just set up drop-off points
actions.resultExplain: Much cheaper Lower take-up
actions.plainMeaning: Place recycling banks around the city for people to use.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

actions.label: Wait until the budget is healthier
actions.resultExplain: Saves money today. You lose early momentum and stay a throwaway city.
actions.plainLabel: Wait until the budget is healthier
actions.plainMeaning: Delay recycling for now.
cons: You lose early momentum and stay a throwaway city.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

justify.question: Why does source separation only work with citizen participation?
options: Contaminated mixed waste can't be recycled efficiently

roundEvents.title: Factory Lines
roundEvents.brief: A new industrial park has opened, bringing jobs and tax money — and a flood of commercial and factory waste that's very different from household rubbish. Some of it is bulky, some hazardous, all of it lands on your disposal system.
roundEvents.caseFact: Urban waste is not just domestic; it comes from industrial and commercial activity too.

actions.label: Require factories to audit and reuse waste
actions.resultExplain: Pushes industry to be circular Some firms complain about red tape.
actions.plainLabel: Require factories to audit and reuse waste
actions.plainMeaning: Make businesses track their waste and reuse what they can.
cons: Some firms complain about red tape.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

actions.label: Charge factories a disposal fee
actions.resultExplain: Raises useful revenue Some firms may cut corners or dump illegally.
actions.plainMeaning: Make businesses pay for what they throw away.
pros: gives a mild nudge to waste less.
cons: Some firms may cut corners or dump illegally.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

actions.label: Take their waste into the general system
actions.resultExplain: Keeps businesses happy and attracts more. Overloads your disposal
actions.plainLabel: Take their waste into the general system
actions.plainMeaning: Handle factory waste alongside household rubbish.
pros: Keeps businesses happy and attracts more.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

justify.question: Why must cities plan for more than domestic rubbish?
options: Industrial and commercial activity generate large, varied waste streams

roundEvents.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

roundEvents.title: Wrap and Throw
roundEvents.brief: With no proper sewers or collection in the poorest districts, people wrap their waste in paper and throw it straight into the river — the "wrap and throw" habit. The water downstream is turning foul, and the wells nearby are no longer safe to drink from.
roundEvents.caseFact: In Manila, human waste is wrapped in newspaper and thrown into rivers — the "wrap and throw" method.

actions.label: Build basic sanitation and collection
actions.resultExplain: Fixes the real cause Expensive and takes time to build.
actions.plainMeaning: Give these districts toilets, sewers and rubbish pickup.
cons: Expensive and takes time to build.
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

actions.label: Clean the river and run awareness drives
actions.resultExplain: Visible quick win The waste still needs somewhere proper to go.
actions.plainLabel: Clean the river and run awareness drives
actions.plainMeaning: Clear the waterway and teach people why dumping harms them.
cons: The waste still needs somewhere proper to go.
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

actions.label: Tolerate it
actions.resultExplain: Costs nothing now. Wells and the sea get poisoned
actions.plainMeaning: Do nothing about the river dumping.
cons: Wells and the sea get poisoned
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

justify.question: Why does dumping in rivers harm areas far away?
options: Waste and leachate travel downstream and to the sea, spreading contamination

roundEvents.scene: With no proper sewers or collection in the poorest districts, people wrap their waste in paper and throw it straight into the river — the "wrap and throw" habit. The water downstream is turning foul, and the wells nearby are no longer safe to drink from.

roundEvents.title: Teach the Future
roundEvents.brief: Teachers want to bring waste and recycling into every classroom — turning kids into the city's keenest sorters and gentle nags at home. It won't show up in this year's numbers, but it could change the city's habits for a generation.
roundEvents.caseFact: Changing public attitudes — linking waste to climate and making sorting logical — underpins Copenhagen's success.

actions.label: City-wide schools programme
actions.resultExplain: Builds lasting recycling habits cheaply Slow to show in the metrics.
actions.plainMeaning: Teach every school about reducing and sorting waste.
cons: Slow to show in the metrics.
actions.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

actions.label: One-off advertising campaign
actions.resultExplain: Cheap Limited reach
actions.plainMeaning: Run a short media push on recycling.
actions.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

actions.label: Skip it
actions.resultExplain: No cost. Habits never shift
actions.plainMeaning: Don't invest in education.
actions.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

justify.question: Why invest in attitudes, not just bins?

roundEvents.scene: Teachers want to bring waste and recycling into every classroom — turning kids into the city's keenest sorters and gentle nags at home. It won't show up in this year's numbers, but it could change the city's habits for a generation.

roundEvents.title: Digital Discards
roundEvents.brief: Old phones, laptops and chargers are piling up as people upgrade constantly. This "e-waste" is tricky — it's full of toxic metals, but also valuable materials worth recovering. Right now most of it is just getting tossed in with normal rubbish.
roundEvents.caseFact: Electronics rely on rare-earth and critical raw materials; e-waste contains toxic and valuable components.

actions.label: Set up e-waste take-back and recovery
actions.resultExplain: Recovers precious materials and income Costs to set up specialist handling.
actions.plainLabel: Set up e-waste take-back and recovery
actions.plainMeaning: Collect old electronics and recover the valuable metals inside.
pros: keeps toxins out of the ground.
cons: Costs to set up specialist handling.
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

actions.label: Open a hazardous-waste drop-off
actions.resultExplain: Stops the worst contamination Recovers less of the value.
actions.plainMeaning: Give people a safe place to hand in dangerous items.
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

actions.label: Mix it into normal rubbish
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest, no effort. Toxic metals leak into soil and water
actions.plainMeaning: Treat e-waste like any other waste.
cons: Toxic metals leak into soil and water
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

justify.question: Why treat e-waste as a resource, not rubbish?
options: It contains recoverable critical materials and toxic substances that must be contained

roundEvents.scene: Old phones, laptops and chargers are piling up as people upgrade constantly. This "e-waste" is tricky — it's full of toxic metals, but also valuable materials worth recovering. Right now most of it is just getting tossed in with normal rubbish.
roundEvents.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

roundEvents.title: The Last Cell
roundEvents.brief: The city's main rubbish tip is nearly full — engineers reckon it has less than a year left. Finding new land near a crowded city is hard and unpopular, and the trucks keep rolling in every day. You need a plan before the gates are forced shut.
roundEvents.caseFact: Cities are running out of landfill space; finding suitable land near dense urban areas is increasingly hard.

actions.label: Build a proper sealed landfill
actions.resultExplain: Buys safe disposal space Expensive
actions.plainMeaning: Construct a modern, lined tip that won't leak into the environment.
pros: far cleaner than an open tip.
cons: the space is still finite and will fill again.
actions.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

actions.label: Open a cheap open dump
actions.resultExplain: Instant relief Poisons land, air and water
actions.plainMeaning: Just start tipping rubbish on open ground.
actions.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

actions.label: Cut the rubbish coming in with recycling
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the real problem Slower
actions.plainLabel: Cut the rubbish coming in with recycling
actions.plainMeaning: Reduce how much waste needs burying in the first place.
actions.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

justify.question: Why is "just build another dump" a trap?
options: Land is finite and dumps create lasting health

roundEvents.scene: The city's main rubbish tip is nearly full — engineers reckon it has less than a year left. Finding new land near a crowded city is hard and unpopular, and the trucks keep rolling in every day. You need a plan before the gates are forced shut.
roundEvents.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

roundEvents.title: The Cheap Option
roundEvents.brief: Budget hawks on the council are pushing hard for the cheapest fix: just dump it on open land and move on. It would save a fortune this year. But you've seen what open dumps do to the people who live near them.
roundEvents.caseFact: Over 90% of waste in low-income countries is openly dumped or burned, with serious consequences.

actions.label: Refuse — pay for a proper sealed tip
actions.resultExplain: Protects residents Strains the budget now.
actions.plainLabel: Refuse — pay for a proper sealed tip
actions.plainMeaning: Spend more to protect health and the environment.
actions.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

actions.label: Approve the open dump
actions.resultExplain: Big budget saving Severe pollution and health damage
actions.plainMeaning: Take the cheapest route.
cons: a problem that grows every year.
actions.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

actions.label: Compromise: controlled tipping with soil cover
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper than full engineering Still leaks and pollutes more than a sealed site.
actions.plainLabel: Compromise: controlled tipping with soil cover
actions.plainMeaning: A halfway option — cover the rubbish daily but skip full engineering.
cons: Still leaks and pollutes more than a sealed site.
actions.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

justify.question: What's the core difference between a dump and an engineered landfill?
options: Lining, leachate treatment and controls that limit contamination

roundEvents.scene: Budget hawks on the council are pushing hard for the cheapest fix: just dump it on open land and move on. It would save a fortune this year. But you've seen what open dumps do to the people who live near them.
roundEvents.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

roundEvents.title: Island of Last Resort
roundEvents.brief: Engineers pitch something bold: a landfill built offshore, ringed with clay and waterproof lining, with its own plant to treat the toxic runoff so the sea stays clean. It's world-class — and it comes with a world-class price tag.
roundEvents.caseFact: Semakau (1999, SGD$610m) is lined with membrane, marine clay and rock; leachate is treated to keep waters clean.

actions.label: Fund the full sealed island tip
actions.resultExplain: Best protection for the sea and groundwater Heavy debt that drains future budgets.
actions.plainLabel: Fund the full sealed island tip
actions.plainMeaning: Build it properly, borrowing money to do so.
pros: Best protection for the sea and groundwater
cons: Heavy debt that drains future budgets.
actions.conceptLink: a well-engineered landfill (the Semakau model) and why lining matters.

actions.label: Build a cheaper unlined version
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper Toxic runoff threatens groundwater and the sea.
actions.plainMeaning: Skip the expensive lining to save money.
cons: Toxic runoff threatens groundwater and the sea.
actions.conceptLink: a well-engineered landfill (the Semakau model) and why lining matters.

actions.label: Reject it — push recycling instead
actions.resultExplain: No giant tip needed if you can reduce enough Risky if recycling can't keep up with the waste.
actions.plainMeaning: Avoid the landfill by cutting waste volume.
pros: No giant tip needed if you can reduce enough
cons: Risky if recycling can't keep up with the waste.
actions.conceptLink: a well-engineered landfill (the Semakau model) and why lining matters.

justify.question: Why does lining/leachate treatment matter most when a landfill sits above an aquifer?
options: Leachate can contaminate groundwater used by people

roundEvents.scene: Engineers pitch something bold: a landfill built offshore, ringed with clay and waterproof lining, with its own plant to treat the toxic runoff so the sea stays clean. It's world-class — and it comes with a world-class price tag.
roundEvents.conceptLink: a well-engineered landfill (the Semakau model) and why lining matters.

roundEvents.title: Not in Our Backyard, Either
roundEvents.brief: For years you've trucked your rubbish out to a rural town that quietly took it. Now that town has had enough — its people are blockading the road, sick of bearing your city's waste. The lorries are backing up.
roundEvents.caseFact: Collected waste often ends in landfills far from the city, creating "waste hinterlands" that bear the burden.

actions.label: Negotiate a fair deal with the town
actions.resultExplain: Keeps disposal flowing Costs money to compensate them.
actions.plainLabel: Negotiate a fair deal with the town
actions.plainMeaning: Share benefits and compensation so they'll keep helping.
actions.conceptLink: "waste hinterlands" — the countryside that carries the city's burden.

actions.label: Truck it even further away
actions.resultExplain: Solves it quickly without a fight. More fuel, more emissions, higher cost — and the same problem elsewhere.
actions.plainMeaning: Find a more distant site to dump in.
pros: Solves it quickly without a fight.
cons: More fuel, more emissions, higher cost — and the same problem elsewhere.
actions.conceptLink: "waste hinterlands" — the countryside that carries the city's burden.

actions.label: Send them less by recycling more
actions.resultExplain: Eases the pressure for good Costs money and takes time to ramp up.
actions.plainLabel: Send them less by recycling more
actions.plainMeaning: Shrink the waste you export.
cons: Costs money and takes time to ramp up.
actions.conceptLink: "waste hinterlands" — the countryside that carries the city's burden.

justify.question: Why is exporting waste to a hinterland not a real solution?
options: It shifts the environmental burden elsewhere without reducing it

roundEvents.title: Poison in the Water
roundEvents.brief: Tests come back grim: a dark, toxic liquid is seeping out of your rubbish site and creeping toward the wells families drink from. Doctors are warning of risks to pregnant women and babies. The clock is ticking.
roundEvents.caseFact: Studies link living near landfills to raised risks of birth defects from contaminated water and soil.

actions.label: Build a runoff treatment plant
actions.resultExplain: Stops the contamination directly Expensive to build and run.
actions.plainMeaning: Capture and clean the toxic liquid before it spreads.
actions.conceptLink: leachate — the poisonous liquid that drips from rotting waste.

actions.label: Hand out bottled water for now
actions.resultExplain: Cheap Doesn't fix anything
actions.plainLabel: Hand out bottled water for now
actions.plainMeaning: A stopgap while you decide what to do.
actions.conceptLink: leachate — the poisonous liquid that drips from rotting waste.

actions.label: Deny it and just monitor
actions.resultExplain: Costs almost nothing now. People get sick
actions.plainMeaning: Downplay the problem and watch it.
actions.conceptLink: leachate — the poisonous liquid that drips from rotting waste.

justify.question: What is leachate?
options: Liquid that percolates through waste, carrying contaminants

roundEvents.title: Burn or Bury?

It sounds like magic — but the neighbours near the proposed site are already nervous about the smoke.

actions.label: Build a big modern incinerator
actions.resultExplain: Massively cuts the rubbish pile Air pollution and health worries

actions.label: Build a smaller waste-to-energy plant

cons: Handles less waste, so capacity relief is smaller.
actions.conceptLink: the central trade-offs of incineration.

actions.label: Don't burn — expand recycling instead

cons: Slower to relieve the capacity crunch

roundEvents.title: The Danish Model

roundEvents.title: Close the Hole
roundEvents.brief: You can ban dumping anything that could be recycled or burned instead — forcing the city to choose better options. It only works because you've built the alternatives; now you can shut the easy escape hatch.
roundEvents.caseFact: Denmark made it illegal to landfill waste that can be incinerated.

actions.label: Ban landfilling of recyclables and combustibles
actions.resultExplain: Forces waste up the hierarchy Only works if you've built the alternatives.
actions.plainLabel: Ban landfilling of recyclables and combustibles
actions.plainMeaning: Forbid dumping anything that can be recovered.
cons: Only works if you've built the alternatives.
actions.conceptLink: banning landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste.

actions.label: Phase the ban in by material
actions.resultExplain: A smoother, gradual transition. Slower to take effect.
actions.plainLabel: Phase the ban in by material
actions.plainMeaning: Ban materials one at a time.
actions.conceptLink: banning landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste.

actions.label: No ban
actions.resultExplain: Keeps the easy option open. Circular progress stalls.
actions.plainMeaning: Keep landfilling allowed.
actions.conceptLink: banning landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste.

justify.question: Why ban landfilling of recyclables/combustibles?
options: It forces higher-value treatment and saves scarce land

roundEvents.scene: You can ban dumping anything that could be recycled or burned instead — forcing the city to choose better options. It only works because you've built the alternatives; now you can shut the easy escape hatch.

roundEvents.title: The Circular Workforce
roundEvents.brief: A circular economy needs people who can repair, sort, recover and remanufacture — so you can invest in training a whole new green workforce. It's a chance to make sustainability a source of good jobs.
roundEvents.caseFact: Circular activities (repair, recovery, remanufacture) create local employment.

actions.label: Launch a city-wide green-skills programme
actions.resultExplain: Builds the workforce circularity needs Training costs.
actions.plainMeaning: Train residents for repair, recycling and remanufacturing jobs.
actions.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

actions.label: Subsidise circular start-ups
actions.resultExplain: Seeds innovation. Narrower than broad training.
actions.plainMeaning: Fund new businesses in the circular economy.
actions.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

actions.label: No programme
actions.resultExplain: No spend. The transition lacks skilled workers.
actions.plainMeaning: Don't invest in skills.
actions.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

justify.question: Why does a circular economy need workforce investment?

roundEvents.scene: A circular economy needs people who can repair, sort, recover and remanufacture — so you can invest in training a whole new green workforce. It's a chance to make sustainability a source of good jobs.
roundEvents.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

roundEvents.title: Make Makers Pay
roundEvents.brief: Why should the city foot the bill for disposing of products companies sell? An "extended producer responsibility" law would make manufacturers pay to handle their goods at end of life — and design them to be recyclable in the first place.
roundEvents.caseFact: Polluter-Pays logic extends to producers funding the disposal of what they sell.

actions.label: Bring in full producer responsibility
actions.resultExplain: Shifts cost and design incentives onto producers Industry friction.
actions.plainMeaning: Make manufacturers fund the end-of-life of what they sell.
pros: Shifts cost and design incentives onto producers
actions.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

actions.label: Apply it to packaging and electronics only
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the biggest problems. Narrower scope.
actions.plainLabel: Apply it to packaging and electronics only
actions.plainMeaning: Target the worst waste streams first.
actions.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

actions.label: Decline
actions.resultExplain: Avoids industry fights. The city keeps paying the bill.
actions.plainMeaning: Don't introduce EPR.
cons: The city keeps paying the bill.
actions.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

justify.question: What does EPR change?
options: Producers bear end-of-life costs, incentivising recyclable design

roundEvents.scene: Why should the city foot the bill for disposing of products companies sell? An "extended producer responsibility" law would make manufacturers pay to handle their goods at end of life — and design them to be recyclable in the first place.
roundEvents.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

roundEvents.title: Putting It to the People
roundEvents.brief: You decide to put a new waste levy to a public vote — a real test of whether the city trusts you. If you've earned their faith over the years, they'll back it; if not, this could backfire.
roundEvents.caseFact: Public attitudes and buy-in are decisive for waste policy success.

actions.label: Campaign honestly for the levy
actions.resultExplain: Wins lasting public consent Risky if trust is already low.
actions.plainMeaning: Make the open, transparent case to voters.
cons: Risky if trust is already low.
actions.conceptLink: public trust and participation in waste policy.

actions.label: Water it down to ensure it passes
actions.resultExplain: Likely to pass. Weaker measure, smaller impact.
actions.plainLabel: Water it down to ensure it passes
actions.plainMeaning: Offer a weaker version voters will accept.
actions.conceptLink: public trust and participation in waste policy.

actions.label: Skip the vote and impose it
actions.resultExplain: Fast. Deepens distrust and future resistance.
actions.plainLabel: Skip the vote and impose it
actions.plainMeaning: Bring in the levy without asking.
actions.conceptLink: public trust and participation in waste policy.

justify.question: Why does public trust shape what policy is possible?
options: Citizens must consent to and participate in waste schemes for them to work

roundEvents.scene: You decide to put a new waste levy to a public vote — a real test of whether the city trusts you. If you've earned their faith over the years, they'll back it; if not, this could backfire.
roundEvents.conceptLink: public trust and participation in waste policy.

roundEvents.title: Pay As You Throw
roundEvents.brief: New technology lets you charge households by exactly how much rubbish they throw out, with smart bins tracking it. People who waste less pay less — a direct, personal incentive to cut down. You just have to keep it fair.
roundEvents.caseFact: Pricing waste by volume creates direct incentives to reduce and sort.

actions.label: Roll out smart bins and pay-as-you-throw
actions.resultExplain: Strong personal incentive to reduce and sort Must be designed carefully so it's fair to poorer households.
actions.plainLabel: Roll out smart bins and pay-as-you-throw
actions.plainMeaning: Charge each household by how much waste they produce.
pros: Strong personal incentive to reduce and sort
cons: Must be designed carefully so it's fair to poorer households.
actions.conceptLink: pay-as-you-throw pricing and smart waste tech.

actions.label: Use smart bins for monitoring only
actions.resultExplain: Improves logistics. Doesn't change behaviour much.
actions.plainLabel: Use smart bins for monitoring only
actions.plainMeaning: Track waste without charging by volume.
actions.conceptLink: pay-as-you-throw pricing and smart waste tech.

actions.label: No new tech
actions.resultExplain: No spend. Misses the data and incentive gains.
actions.plainMeaning: Carry on without it.
cons: Misses the data and incentive gains.
actions.conceptLink: pay-as-you-throw pricing and smart waste tech.

justify.question: Why does pay-as-you-throw cut waste?
options: Charging by volume gives households a direct reason to reduce and sort

roundEvents.scene: New technology lets you charge households by exactly how much rubbish they throw out, with smart bins tracking it. People who waste less pay less — a direct, personal incentive to cut down. You just have to keep it fair.
roundEvents.conceptLink: pay-as-you-throw pricing and smart waste tech.

roundEvents.title: The Final Audit
roundEvents.brief: As your term ends, an independent review weighs up what kind of city you've built — one that treats waste as a resource and thrives, or one still stuck in the throwaway mindset. The verdict will define your legacy.
roundEvents.caseFact: Optimistic "model" cities adopt circular metabolism; pessimistic ones stay linear and unsustainable.

actions.label: Publish the results and commit to keep improving
actions.resultExplain: Cements a circular path and accountability Invites scrutiny
actions.plainLabel: Publish the results and commit to keep improving
actions.plainMeaning: Be transparent and lock in continuous improvement.
pros: Cements a circular path and accountability
pays off most for circular cities.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city as the city's lasting identity.

actions.label: Hold a quiet internal review
actions.resultExplain: Low effort. Little momentum or learning.
actions.plainMeaning: Keep the review low-key.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city as the city's lasting identity.

actions.label: Declare success and move on
actions.resultExplain: Avoids scrutiny. No learning
actions.plainMeaning: Skip honest reflection.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city as the city's lasting identity.

justify.question: What separates a circular "model" city from a linear one?
options: Reducing inputs and recycling outputs back into the system vs treating waste as rubbish

roundEvents.scene: As your term ends, an independent review weighs up what kind of city you've built — one that treats waste as a resource and thrives, or one still stuck in the throwaway mindset. The verdict will define your legacy.
roundEvents.conceptLink: linear vs circular city as the city's lasting identity.

1.title: The Convenience Trap
1.brief: Walk down any high street and everything is wrapped in plastic — fruit in trays, drinks in double layers, snacks in pouches inside boxes. Shops love it because shiny packaging sells, but your bins are overflowing overnight. Residents are starting to notice the mountains of wrapping on collection day.
1.caseFact: Refrigeration and global transport made convenience packaging routine — packaging is now a marketing tool, not just protection.

actions.label: Charge a small fee on excess packaging
actions.resultExplain: Cuts rubbish at the source Shops and shoppers grumble about higher prices
actions.plainLabel: Charge a small fee on excess packaging
actions.plainMeaning: Make shops pay a little for wasteful wrapping, nudging them to use less.
cons: Shops and shoppers grumble about higher prices
the benefit takes time to show.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

actions.label: Run a 'bring your own bag/cup' campaign
actions.resultExplain: Cheap and popular Only some people join in
actions.plainLabel: Run a 'bring your own bag/cup' campaign
actions.plainMeaning: Encourage people to refuse single-use packaging voluntarily.
cons: the effect is small and slow.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

actions.label: Do nothing, just collect more
actions.resultExplain: No backlash Rubbish keeps climbing
actions.plainMeaning: Leave shops alone and keep picking up the extra rubbish.
pros: keeps shops and shoppers happy today.
cons: you've locked in a throwaway culture.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

justify.question: Why is reducing packaging "better" than recycling it?
options: It prevents the waste and its disposal cost from ever existing

Shops love it because shiny packaging sells, but your bins are overflowing overnight. Residents are starting to notice the mountains of wrapping on collection day.
1.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

1.title: Built to be Binned
1.brief: Cheap gadgets and clothes are everywhere, and they're designed to be tossed, not fixed — when a phone battery dies, it's cheaper to buy a new phone than repair it. Landfills are filling with stuff that barely got used. People shrug; that's just how things are now.
1.caseFact: Many industries rely on built-in obsolescence — replacement is cheaper than repair, driving constant waste.

actions.label: Fund repair cafés and reuse shops
actions.resultExplain: Extends the life of products Costs money up front
actions.plainLabel: Fund repair cafés and reuse shops
actions.plainMeaning: Set up places where people can fix and re-sell things instead of binning them.
cons: only works if people choose to use them.
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

actions.label: Pass a 'right to repair' rule
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the root cause Manufacturers lobby hard against it
actions.plainMeaning: Force makers to design products that can actually be fixed.
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

actions.label: Just collect the extra rubbish
actions.resultExplain: Keeps streets tidy now Treats the symptom, not the cause
actions.plainMeaning: Expand collection to cope with all the discarded goods.
cons: Treats the symptom, not the cause
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

justify.question: What does "built-in obsolescence" do to a city's waste stream?
options: Continuously enlarges it as products are replaced not repaired

1.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

1.title: Lockdown Leftovers
1.brief: Food delivery has exploded — every meal arrives in a stack of plastic tubs, bags and cutlery. In just two months the city has buried the equivalent of dozens of bus-loads of extra plastic. It's convenient, but the bins tell the story.
1.caseFact: Singapore households generated +1,334 tonnes of plastic during a two-month lockdown from deliveries.

actions.label: Launch a reusable container scheme
actions.resultExplain: Cuts packaging waste for good Needs a return-and-wash system to work
actions.plainMeaning: Set up returnable tubs so meals don't need throwaway packaging.
cons: Needs a return-and-wash system to work
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

actions.label: Add recycling bins at food courts
actions.resultExplain: Captures materials if people sort right Lots of food-soiled plastic can't be recycled
actions.plainLabel: Add recycling bins at food courts
actions.plainMeaning: Put clearly-labelled bins where the takeaway waste is created.
pros: Captures materials if people sort right
cons: Lots of food-soiled plastic can't be recycled
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

actions.label: Send it all to the tip
actions.resultExplain: No effort or cost now. Plastic lasts centuries
actions.plainLabel: Send it all to the tip
actions.plainMeaning: Just bury the extra packaging.
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

justify.question: Why did delivery culture raise the footprint so fast?
options: More packaging per meal, mostly non-recyclable plastic

1.title: Edge City
1.brief: A brand-new neighbourhood is rising at the city's edge — homes are filling up before the bin lorries even have a route out there. Residents are already leaving bags on the kerb with nowhere for them to go. You can shape how this suburb deals with waste from day one.
1.caseFact: Urban sprawl pushes infrastructure outward; fringe areas often lack basic services first.

actions.label: Build full sorting + collection from day one
actions.resultExplain: Designs good habits in from the start Most expensive option up front.
actions.plainLabel: Build full sorting + collection from day one
actions.plainMeaning: Give the new suburb proper recycling and collection immediately.
pros: Designs good habits in from the start
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

actions.label: Basic bin collection only
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper Locks in throwaway habits
actions.plainMeaning: Just send lorries to pick up mixed rubbish.
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

actions.label: Let residents sort it out themselves
actions.resultExplain: Saves money today. Illegal dumping and resentment grow
actions.plainLabel: Let residents sort it out themselves
actions.plainMeaning: Leave the new suburb to manage its own waste for now.
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

justify.question: Why design waste systems before a district is built?
options: Retrofitting habits and infrastructure later is far harder and costlier
It is required by law everywhere

1.title: Streets of Refuse
1.brief: In the older, crowded districts the bin lorries simply can't keep up. Rubbish piles on corners, sometimes catching fire in the heat, and rats and flies are moving in. Families living there are getting sick, and they're angry.
1.caseFact: The 2012 World Bank report found 30–60% of urban waste in lower-income countries is uncollected.

actions.label: Invest in more lorries and routes
actions.resultExplain: Quickly removes the health hazard Costs money
actions.plainLabel: Invest in more lorries and routes
actions.plainMeaning: Expand the collection fleet to clear the backlog.
cons: you still have to dispose of all that waste somewhere.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

actions.label: Hire local informal collectors
actions.resultExplain: Cheap and fast Needs organising and support to be safe and fair.
actions.plainMeaning: Pay the people already collecting waste by cart to do it officially.
pros: boosts recycling and gives people fair work
cons: Needs organising and support to be safe and fair.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

actions.label: Leave it for now
actions.resultExplain: Saves money this year. Disease spreads
actions.plainMeaning: Don't act; focus the budget elsewhere.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

justify.question: Why is uncollected waste a health emergency, not just an eyesore?
options: It breeds disease vectors and contaminates water

1.title: Sort It Out
1.brief: Your team proposes a city-wide scheme: give every household separate bins for paper, glass, plastic and food scraps, and make sorting the easy, normal thing to do. It could transform how the city handles waste — if people get on board.
1.caseFact: Copenhagen made source separation an easy default at household level for paper, glass, metal, plastic, organics.

actions.label: Full kerbside sorting for every home
actions.resultExplain: Big jump in recycling Expensive to launch
actions.plainLabel: Full kerbside sorting for every home
actions.plainMeaning: Roll out separate bins citywide and collect each stream.
cons: flops if households don't sort properly.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

actions.label: Just set up drop-off points
actions.resultExplain: Much cheaper Lower take-up
actions.plainMeaning: Place recycling banks around the city for people to use.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

actions.label: Wait until the budget is healthier
actions.resultExplain: Saves money today. You lose early momentum and stay a throwaway city.
actions.plainLabel: Wait until the budget is healthier
actions.plainMeaning: Delay recycling for now.
cons: You lose early momentum and stay a throwaway city.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

justify.question: Why does source separation only work with citizen participation?
options: Contaminated mixed waste can't be recycled efficiently

1.title: Factory Lines
1.brief: A new industrial park has opened, bringing jobs and tax money — and a flood of commercial and factory waste that's very different from household rubbish. Some of it is bulky, some hazardous, all of it lands on your disposal system.
1.caseFact: Urban waste is not just domestic; it comes from industrial and commercial activity too.

actions.label: Require factories to audit and reuse waste
actions.resultExplain: Pushes industry to be circular Some firms complain about red tape.
actions.plainLabel: Require factories to audit and reuse waste
actions.plainMeaning: Make businesses track their waste and reuse what they can.
cons: Some firms complain about red tape.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

actions.label: Charge factories a disposal fee
actions.resultExplain: Raises useful revenue Some firms may cut corners or dump illegally.
actions.plainMeaning: Make businesses pay for what they throw away.
pros: gives a mild nudge to waste less.
cons: Some firms may cut corners or dump illegally.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

actions.label: Take their waste into the general system
actions.resultExplain: Keeps businesses happy and attracts more. Overloads your disposal
actions.plainLabel: Take their waste into the general system
actions.plainMeaning: Handle factory waste alongside household rubbish.
pros: Keeps businesses happy and attracts more.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

justify.question: Why must cities plan for more than domestic rubbish?
options: Industrial and commercial activity generate large, varied waste streams

1.title: Wrap and Throw
1.brief: With no proper sewers or collection in the poorest districts, people wrap their waste in paper and throw it straight into the river — the "wrap and throw" habit. The water downstream is turning foul, and the wells nearby are no longer safe to drink from.
1.caseFact: In Manila, human waste is wrapped in newspaper and thrown into rivers — the "wrap and throw" method.

actions.label: Build basic sanitation and collection
actions.resultExplain: Fixes the real cause Expensive and takes time to build.
actions.plainMeaning: Give these districts toilets, sewers and rubbish pickup.
cons: Expensive and takes time to build.
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

actions.label: Clean the river and run awareness drives
actions.resultExplain: Visible quick win The waste still needs somewhere proper to go.
actions.plainLabel: Clean the river and run awareness drives
actions.plainMeaning: Clear the waterway and teach people why dumping harms them.
cons: The waste still needs somewhere proper to go.
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

actions.label: Tolerate it
actions.resultExplain: Costs nothing now. Wells and the sea get poisoned
actions.plainMeaning: Do nothing about the river dumping.
cons: Wells and the sea get poisoned
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

justify.question: Why does dumping in rivers harm areas far away?

1.brief: Teachers want to bring waste and recycling into every classroom — turning kids into the city's keenest sorters and gentle nags at home. It won't show up in this year's numbers, but it could change the city's habits for a generation.

actions.label: City-wide schools programme

actions.label: One-off advertising campaign
actions.resultExplain: Cheap Limited reach
actions.plainMeaning: Run a short media push on recycling.

actions.label: Skip it
actions.resultExplain: No cost. Habits never shift
actions.plainMeaning: Don't invest in education.

1.title: Digital Discards
1.brief: Old phones, laptops and chargers are piling up as people upgrade constantly. This "e-waste" is tricky — it's full of toxic metals, but also valuable materials worth recovering. Right now most of it is just getting tossed in with normal rubbish.
1.caseFact: Electronics rely on rare-earth and critical raw materials; e-waste contains toxic and valuable components.

actions.label: Set up e-waste take-back and recovery
actions.resultExplain: Recovers precious materials and income Costs to set up specialist handling.
actions.plainLabel: Set up e-waste take-back and recovery
actions.plainMeaning: Collect old electronics and recover the valuable metals inside.
pros: keeps toxins out of the ground.
cons: Costs to set up specialist handling.
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

actions.label: Open a hazardous-waste drop-off
actions.resultExplain: Stops the worst contamination Recovers less of the value.
actions.plainMeaning: Give people a safe place to hand in dangerous items.
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

actions.label: Mix it into normal rubbish
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest, no effort. Toxic metals leak into soil and water
actions.plainMeaning: Treat e-waste like any other waste.
cons: Toxic metals leak into soil and water
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

justify.question: Why treat e-waste as a resource, not rubbish?
options: It contains recoverable critical materials and toxic substances that must be contained

2.title: The Last Cell

cons: the space is still finite and will fill again.

actions.label: Open a cheap open dump
actions.resultExplain: Instant relief Poisons land, air and water

2.brief: Budget hawks on the council are pushing hard for the cheapest fix: just dump it on open land and move on. It would save a fortune this year. But you've seen what open dumps do to the people who live near them.

actions.label: Refuse — pay for a proper sealed tip

actions.plainLabel: Refuse — pay for a proper sealed tip
actions.plainMeaning: Spend more to protect health and the environment.

6.title: The Danish Model
